text: You guys have no idea of the iceberg of vaccine fear you get growing up in an antivax environment. As the only person in my family who got the covid shot, let me give you a tour.

raw.full_text: You guys have no idea of the iceberg of vaccine fear you get growing up in an antivax environment. As the only person in my family who got the covid shot, let me give you a tour.

text: The tip of the iceberg is straightforward adverse effects. "Vaccines cause autism" This is where the fact checkers wage war. It's easy to debunk the most egregious claims but adverse effects are actually a real thing and denying that only polarizes everyone.

raw.full_text: The tip of the iceberg is straightforward adverse effects. "Vaccines cause autism" This is where the fact checkers wage war. It's easy to debunk the most egregious claims but adverse effects are actually a real thing and denying that only polarizes everyone.

text: It's easy to check the facts, but harder to shake the lingering feeling that maybe we're missing more subtle side effects in an environment of mutual distrust. Which feeds into the second layer...

raw.full_text: It's easy to check the facts, but harder to shake the lingering feeling that maybe we're missing more subtle side effects in an environment of mutual distrust. Which feeds into the second layer...

text: Big pharma conspiracy theories. Again there are super egregious claims like injecting 5G chips, but there is no denying that big pharma funds research and is incentivised to sell more vaccines. Saying "trust the science" doesn't help, earn our trust with honesty and transparency

raw.full_text: Big pharma conspiracy theories. Again there are super egregious claims like injecting 5G chips, but there is no denying that big pharma funds research and is incentivised to sell more vaccines. Saying "trust the science" doesn't help, earn our trust with honesty and transparency

text: There is very little middle ground here, it's either batshit insane conspiracy theories and pseudoscience or obviously false claims that every single vaccine is perfectly safe and good. Of course nobody is crossing the chasm if the other side is clearly lying.

raw.full_text: There is very little middle ground here, it's either batshit insane conspiracy theories and pseudoscience or obviously false claims that every single vaccine is perfectly safe and good. Of course nobody is crossing the chasm if the other side is clearly lying.

text: On these two layers I've become a bit of a fan of Peter Gøtzsche, who equally debunks antivax myths, and questions official recommendations based on bad science. 5/7 would recommend. But we're far from done...

text: The question each such project must ask is, who is the intended audience? What is it meant to survive? Is it us or aliens? What is their level of technology? How easy is it to access? What do you want to tell them?

raw.full_text: The question each such project must ask is, who is the intended audience? What is it meant to survive? Is it us or aliens? What is their level of technology? How easy is it to access? What do you want to tell them?

text: You can bury it in the ground, launch it into space, distribute it all over the world... You can make golden vinyls, build regular old libraries, print microscopic films, etch holographic crystals, bake clay tablets... You can use text, binary, or pictures...

raw.full_text: You can bury it in the ground, launch it into space, distribute it all over the world... You can make golden vinyls, build regular old libraries, print microscopic films, etch holographic crystals, bake clay tablets... You can use text, binary, or pictures...

text: The thing is, you get the best storage density and durability by making it completely unreadable by the naked eye and inaccessible by a guy with a shovel. Eg launch binary micro film into space. On the other hand you can walk into a library right now and read anything.

raw.full_text: The thing is, you get the best storage density and durability by making it completely unreadable by the naked eye and inaccessible by a guy with a shovel. Eg launch binary micro film into space. On the other hand you can walk into a library right now and read anything.

text: Honestly if our civilization doesn't end in a flood or fire, regular old libraries are probably the most effective means of preservation we have. Clay tablets are just that but fireproof and even lower density.

raw.full_text: Honestly if our civilization doesn't end in a flood or fire, regular old libraries are probably the most effective means of preservation we have. Clay tablets are just that but fireproof and even lower density.

text: Another big question is what you want to preserve. Open source code, the classics, a historical record, art, Wikipedia, memes, literally everything... I started at Wikipedia but then moved on to poetry and memes because it was more fun.

raw.full_text: Another big question is what you want to preserve. Open source code, the classics, a historical record, art, Wikipedia, memes, literally everything... I started at Wikipedia but then moved on to poetry and memes because it was more fun.

text: Extremely silly things going on in Apicula. I'm trying to document which primitives we support, but while we have a very detailed database about the hardware, the hdl "primitives" are mapped onto those in sometimes nontrivial ways in hardcoded compiler passes.

raw.full_text: Extremely silly things going on in Apicula. I'm trying to document which primitives we support, but while we have a very detailed database about the hardware, the hdl "primitives" are mapped onto those in sometimes nontrivial ways in hardcoded compiler passes.

text: Like there is a LUT8 primitive which is really a tree of LUT4 and MUX2. There are block ram primitives of various widths some of which map to two hardware block rams. There are primitives like LUT2 or DFFE that are just more advanced primitives with unused ports.

raw.full_text: Like there is a LUT8 primitive which is really a tree of LUT4 and MUX2. There are block ram primitives of various widths some of which map to two hardware block rams. There are primitives like LUT2 or DFFE that are just more advanced primitives with unused ports.

text: The only way to list what we support is to go manually through the code for each primitive, ask an AI and pray it gets it right, or just try them all and see which ones error lmao

text: I managed to get vLLM working in Docker! And it's actually faster than Ollama? On qwen2.5:7b-instruct-fp16 vLLM does 36.4 t/s while Ollama does 21.12 t/s. But there is no quantization and it can't load multiple models at the same time.

text:
I might be approaching a point where you can observe SOME useful R-Bus properties. Maybe I could take a break and design a PCB. Three options:
- a passive monitor only
- one R/W port (thermostat)
- two R/W ports (gateway)
The write portion would initially be disabled/bypassed.

raw.full_text:
I might be approaching a point where you can observe SOME useful R-Bus properties. Maybe I could take a break and design a PCB. Three options:
- a passive monitor only
- one R/W port (thermostat)
- two R/W ports (gateway)
The write portion would initially be disabled/bypassed.

text: The monitor is by far the easiest and lowest risk. You're not promising too much. You don't risk breaking the heating. It's just a comparator and an ESP. On the other hand, if you make the gateway, you can in theory do everything with only software updates.

raw.full_text: The monitor is by far the easiest and lowest risk. You're not promising too much. You don't risk breaking the heating. It's just a comparator and an ESP. On the other hand, if you make the gateway, you can in theory do everything with only software updates.

text: The thermostat is like a middle ground. You can just have a disabled output circuit. For the gateway you either need to bypass the two ports, or at least develop an "echo" functionality. At least if you have a working echo, you know that the output actually works well enough.

raw.full_text: The thermostat is like a middle ground. You can just have a disabled output circuit. For the gateway you either need to bypass the two ports, or at least develop an "echo" functionality. At least if you have a working echo, you know that the output actually works well enough.

text: Worst case would be developing a gateway with the output disabled, and then once you're ready to enable it figure out it's not quite correct. Now you over promised and under delivered by a lot.

raw.full_text: Worst case would be developing a gateway with the output disabled, and then once you're ready to enable it figure out it's not quite correct. Now you over promised and under delivered by a lot.

text: I don't think there is huge value in the thermostat tbh. The heater does support opentherm, so the only thing you gain over an opentherm thermostat is that you can see the heat pump energy use and such. At least personally my end goal is a gateway between rtwist and heat pump.

raw.full_text: I don't think there is huge value in the thermostat tbh. The heater does support opentherm, so the only thing you gain over an opentherm thermostat is that you can see the heat pump energy use and such. At least personally my end goal is a gateway between rtwist and heat pump.

text: Or maybe I should just make the gateway in echo mode. I understand the physical layer fairly well, it's just more scary and involved. It's winter and I don't want to disconnect the heating, let alone break it.

raw.full_text: Or maybe I should just make the gateway in echo mode. I understand the physical layer fairly well, it's just more scary and involved. It's winter and I don't want to disconnect the heating, let alone break it.

text: Maybe I'll do the monitor first. Should be cheap and easy and allow monitoring your heat pump. I can whip it up quickly, test it, order a dozen PCBA, throw them on Etsy. Hopefully get some enthusiasts involved, and develop the full gateway.

text: USB-C is all bidirectional right so if I just leave it connected maybe it can actually be used EITHER for powering the board without PCIe, or as a USB 2.0 host port. In that case I might as well leave the existing 3.3V supply to power minor things.

text: The annoying thing about the ATX PS_ON# is that the CM5 has a PWR_BUT pin that has all the correct behaviors internally but no clear output to drive PS_ON# with. Do I need to cook up some logic circuit driven by PWR_BUT AND !CM5_+3.3V??? https://t.co/vGvNBchw2G

text: The board has a 5V CPU fan header the connects to a PWM and TACHO pin on the CM5. I guess the simplest solution would be to route the PWM to a 12V 3-pin "follower" fan header. Or actually add a EMC2301 fan controller with some kernel overlay hacks?

raw.full_text: The board has a 5V CPU fan header the connects to a PWM and TACHO pin on the CM5. I guess the simplest solution would be to route the PWM to a 12V 3-pin "follower" fan header. Or actually add a EMC2301 fan controller with some kernel overlay hacks?

text: The USB-C port shares the 5V line with the ATX port which is a bit sketchy if you'd use both, and in the ATX configuration another USB 2.0 port seems more useful. It's just a bunch of component selection and routing changes I'm not eager to do.

raw.full_text: The USB-C port shares the 5V line with the ATX port which is a bit sketchy if you'd use both, and in the ATX configuration another USB 2.0 port seems more useful. It's just a bunch of component selection and routing changes I'm not eager to do.

text: If you were to build a railway throughout the country, would you find different contractors for the groundwork, rails, overhead lines, etc. or split the work into regions?

raw.full_text: If you were to build a railway throughout the country, would you find different contractors for the groundwork, rails, overhead lines, etc. or split the work into regions?

text: My initial thought was it's better to hire specialists but system engineering is all about managing interfaces, and this approach has miles and miles of interface. It's much easier to hook up the regions.

raw.full_text: My initial thought was it's better to hire specialists but system engineering is all about managing interfaces, and this approach has miles and miles of interface. It's much easier to hook up the regions.

text: Similarly I think it's important to avoid mile long interfaces trying to do everything together in your relationship. It leads to endless bickering about different standards. Optimal interfaces TBD...

raw.full_text: Similarly I think it's important to avoid mile long interfaces trying to do everything together in your relationship. It leads to endless bickering about different standards. Optimal interfaces TBD...

text: If you picture a stereotypical men only household it's running just fine but to very different standards. And evidently it is a common struggle that running a female spec household with male members leads to frustration.

raw.full_text: If you picture a stereotypical men only household it's running just fine but to very different standards. And evidently it is a common struggle that running a female spec household with male members leads to frustration.

text: Willpower is a very scarce resource (new year's resolutions anyone?) so using that to change how you operate is an uphill battle. Ideally you'd find domains where each member excels, alternatively you need to pick your battles to reassign the most gross male spec domains.

raw.full_text: Willpower is a very scarce resource (new year's resolutions anyone?) so using that to change how you operate is an uphill battle. Ideally you'd find domains where each member excels, alternatively you need to pick your battles to reassign the most gross male spec domains.

text: Distributed systems are hard right? It's actually great to have an authoritative source of truth who knows the status of some domain. Communication overhead is one reason you can't have your mythical man month. Emotional labour is good actually, if balanced properly.

raw.full_text: Distributed systems are hard right? It's actually great to have an authoritative source of truth who knows the status of some domain. Communication overhead is one reason you can't have your mythical man month. Emotional labour is good actually, if balanced properly.

text:
Adventures in Home Assistant LLMs:
First I tried the OpenAI extension which relies on function calling which llama.cpp doesn't implement.
It appears that llama-cpp-python does, but I can't get it to work and is extremely CPU bottlenecked.

raw.full_text:
Adventures in Home Assistant LLMs:
First I tried the OpenAI extension which relies on function calling which llama.cpp doesn't implement.
It appears that llama-cpp-python does, but I can't get it to work and is extremely CPU bottlenecked.

text: Home-llm does some sort of client side function calling that relies on a finetuned model or in context examples. It can talk to any OpenAI API so I tried both llama and their finetuned model but I can't get either of them to correctly call functions.

raw.full_text: Home-llm does some sort of client side function calling that relies on a finetuned model or in context examples. It can talk to any OpenAI API so I tried both llama and their finetuned model but I can't get either of them to correctly call functions.

text: I think the python wrapper is just doing python things that utterly bottleneck it on a Pi, but I feel like the Home-llm approach should work in theory. It's just very fiddly since there are so many different function calling conventions.

raw.full_text: I think the python wrapper is just doing python things that utterly bottleneck it on a Pi, but I feel like the Home-llm approach should work in theory. It's just very fiddly since there are so many different function calling conventions.

text: One suspicious bit is that llama.cpp said the prompt format of home-3b isn't supported so it's falling back to chatml which maybe misaligned all the function calling conventions or something

raw.full_text: One suspicious bit is that llama.cpp said the prompt format of home-3b isn't supported so it's falling back to chatml which maybe misaligned all the function calling conventions or something

text: So, I'm kinda on the hunt for LLM runners that support ARM and Vulkan and function calling, which is a short list. It appears MLC-LLM may check all the boxes, for ONE specific model?? So I'm trying to compile that now which is slow and painful.

raw.full_text: So, I'm kinda on the hunt for LLM runners that support ARM and Vulkan and function calling, which is a short list. It appears MLC-LLM may check all the boxes, for ONE specific model?? So I'm trying to compile that now which is slow and painful.

text: Home-llm suggests using text-generation-webui and LocalAI. I don't think the former suppors Vulkan at all, but the later does, and also seems to support function calling. Maybe worth a try.

raw.full_text: Home-llm suggests using text-generation-webui and LocalAI. I don't think the former suppors Vulkan at all, but the later does, and also seems to support function calling. Maybe worth a try.

text: @akharix It'd work but defeat the entire purpose of this project. Normally LLMs are entirely GPU bound so you can have a Pi with a huge GPU that runs basic Home Assistant things on the CPU and launches big GPU tasks for AI. The python wrapper is just doing something dumb I think.

raw.full_text: @akharix It'd work but defeat the entire purpose of this project. Normally LLMs are entirely GPU bound so you can have a Pi with a huge GPU that runs basic Home Assistant things on the CPU and launches big GPU tasks for AI. The python wrapper is just doing something dumb I think.

We got a 2007 Suzuki Swift! It was a long and surprising selection process.

First I chatted with Claude a lot about my requirements and wishes, to create a shortlist of small low maintenance cars.

raw.full_text:
We got a 2007 Suzuki Swift! It was a long and surprising selection process.

First I chatted with Claude a lot about my requirements and wishes, to create a shortlist of small low maintenance cars.

text: Then for a while we just noticed cars around us and messaged each other all the interesting ones. I'd look them up, ask Claude about them, and adjust my list. Too old, too new (rip Suzuki Ignis), too heavy (rip mini), only two doors (rip fiat 500), etc

raw.full_text: Then for a while we just noticed cars around us and messaged each other all the interesting ones. I'd look them up, ask Claude about them, and adjust my list. Too old, too new (rip Suzuki Ignis), too heavy (rip mini), only two doors (rip fiat 500), etc

text:
It's interesting that in a matter of weeks I went from designating cars by their colour to knowing every small car on the road.

Few look as unique as the Twingo but especially the Swift was kind of growing on me.

raw.full_text:
It's interesting that in a matter of weeks I went from designating cars by their colour to knowing every small car on the road.

Few look as unique as the Twingo but especially the Swift was kind of growing on me.

text: Other top contenders were Fiat Panda and Toyota Aygo/Peugeot 107/Citroën C1 which are literally the same car from the same factory!! It's funny to me that Japanese brands have a good reputation and yet they all rebadge each others cars.

raw.full_text: Other top contenders were Fiat Panda and Toyota Aygo/Peugeot 107/Citroën C1 which are literally the same car from the same factory!! It's funny to me that Japanese brands have a good reputation and yet they all rebadge each others cars.

text:
I struggled setting up good marktplaats searches for all our contenders until I let go of specific models and set the following requirements:
- < €6k
- < 25km
- < 1000kg
- automatic 
- 4/5 doors
Which lead to 3 candidates:
- 2012 Panda 
- 2009 107
- 2007 Swift

text: My FPGA projects have pretty much been taken over by other contributors, which is great. They bring something important to the world and have taken a life on their own. I'm not really doing much FPGA stuff at all honestly.

raw.full_text: My FPGA projects have pretty much been taken over by other contributors, which is great. They bring something important to the world and have taken a life on their own. I'm not really doing much FPGA stuff at all honestly.

text: Chip design is a more painful topic. I did a degree. I wrote a schematic editor that nobody uses. I worked on a spice simulator that the company abandoned. I tried to tape out an analog chip with open source tools and just couldn't do it. It's drifting away.

raw.full_text: Chip design is a more painful topic. I did a degree. I wrote a schematic editor that nobody uses. I worked on a spice simulator that the company abandoned. I tried to tape out an analog chip with open source tools and just couldn't do it. It's drifting away.

text: After JH I concluded I would need to get some industry experience under my belt to develop solutions that solve real problems. But I don't really want to work in an NDA-ridden risk-averse industry, and I don't think the industry wants to change. So it's drifting away.

raw.full_text: After JH I concluded I would need to get some industry experience under my belt to develop solutions that solve real problems. But I don't really want to work in an NDA-ridden risk-averse industry, and I don't think the industry wants to change. So it's drifting away.

text: Originally I wasn't even interested in chip design, I wanted to do analog electronics and chips are the last bastion of innovation in analog electronics. I enjoyed designing analog chips but really hated the software. But consider this: what if I just did analog electronics https://t.co/kf1IJiIYXu

text: The good news is that I got a new grant to complete NyanCAD, my schematic editor and simulation environment. The bad new is, well there is no bad news really. I'm just not 100% sure what the correct approach is to attract users.

raw.full_text: The good news is that I got a new grant to complete NyanCAD, my schematic editor and simulation environment. The bad new is, well there is no bad news really. I'm just not 100% sure what the correct approach is to attract users.

text: The original goal was to build a schematic editor for open source chip design. One of the problems is that there are a total of five people in the world doing open source analog chip design, and two of them maintain their own schematic editor.

raw.full_text: The original goal was to build a schematic editor for open source chip design. One of the problems is that there are a total of five people in the world doing open source analog chip design, and two of them maintain their own schematic editor.

text: Really the underlying vision has always been to build the falstad simulator for serious work https://t.co/OMbzl9Ifuz

raw.full_text: Really the underlying vision has always been to build the falstad simulator for serious work https://t.co/OMbzl9Ifuz

text: Or the @worrydream simulator for that matter https://t.co/jN3uROAZU4

raw.full_text: Or the @worrydream simulator for that matter https://t.co/jN3uROAZU4

text: I quite literally need to solve for distribution. Currently NyanCAD is 500MB of conda, including a complete CouchDB installation. But at the core it's really just a few thousand lines of ClojureScript (editor) and Python (simulation)

raw.full_text: I quite literally need to solve for distribution. Currently NyanCAD is 500MB of conda, including a complete CouchDB installation. But at the core it's really just a few thousand lines of ClojureScript (editor) and Python (simulation)

text: Mixing different paradigms was a mistake I think. You need to go full web, full python, or full native. Problem is, spice simulators are native, notebooks are python, and my editor is web. What do?

raw.full_text: Mixing different paradigms was a mistake I think. You need to go full web, full python, or full native. Problem is, spice simulators are native, notebooks are python, and my editor is web. What do?

text: I think web is right for distribution. I want to be able to collaborate, share, and embed schematics. Become the Onshape of electronics. But how do I bring notebooks and simulators to the web? It's going to involve a lot of WebAssembly or cloud hosting.

raw.full_text: I think web is right for distribution. I want to be able to collaborate, share, and embed schematics. Become the Onshape of electronics. But how do I bring notebooks and simulators to the web? It's going to involve a lot of WebAssembly or cloud hosting.

text: I really want to lower the barrier to entry into electronics, so I don't really want to run a SaaS business selling you cloud based simulation. I want you to just... visit the app and start simulating. So... WebAssembly then?

raw.full_text: I really want to lower the barrier to entry into electronics, so I don't really want to run a SaaS business selling you cloud based simulation. I want you to just... visit the app and start simulating. So... WebAssembly then?

text: Let's go over some more design choices and technology choices and see how they panned out, see what alternatives are out there, and what would be worth using/porting/changing. I'm just doing design in the open here, rambling about my thoughts.

raw.full_text: Let's go over some more design choices and technology choices and see how they panned out, see what alternatives are out there, and what would be worth using/porting/changing. I'm just doing design in the open here, rambling about my thoughts.

text: First, just UX devoid of underlying tech. You open the app and get a blank library manager. Garbage. The library manager itself is... alright. The "interface" hierarchy is good for big projects but shouldn't get in the way. subckt editor is garbage.

raw.full_text: First, just UX devoid of underlying tech. You open the app and get a blank library manager. Garbage. The library manager itself is... alright. The "interface" hierarchy is good for big projects but shouldn't get in the way. subckt editor is garbage.

text: Definitely want to open with the editor, and grow with you from there. I never had a good implementation actually working with interfaces, something to think about. subckt port/symbol editor is a can of worms that can spiral into half of inkscape. Auto generate?

raw.full_text: Definitely want to open with the editor, and grow with you from there. I never had a good implementation actually working with interfaces, something to think about. subckt port/symbol editor is a can of worms that can spiral into half of inkscape. Auto generate?

text: The editor itself? Absolute masterpiece. There is a little jank around bulk operations sometimes. There isn't any intelligence around dragging connected things. The simulator link may need rethinking, as it doesn't really do the falstad/worrydream thing that well yet. https://t.co/VpM0Mljn5i

raw.full_text: The editor itself? Absolute masterpiece. There is a little jank around bulk operations sometimes. There isn't any intelligence around dragging connected things. The simulator link may need rethinking, as it doesn't really do the falstad/worrydream thing that well yet. https://t.co/VpM0Mljn5i

text: In the worrydream video he can drag on values to change them which is nice, and then he replaces everything with plots which completely obscures the connectivity, not nice. Real simulations can take time, so some of his interactions may not be realistic.

raw.full_text: In the worrydream video he can drag on values to change them which is nice, and then he replaces everything with plots which completely obscures the connectivity, not nice. Real simulations can take time, so some of his interactions may not be realistic.

text: In the falstad simulator, he uses colored/dotted lines to show voltage and current. Very tangible, but doesn't really show absolute magnitudes well, and doesn't give a great overview across time. This really begs the question if you're making a serious tool or a educational toy.

raw.full_text: In the falstad simulator, he uses colored/dotted lines to show voltage and current. Very tangible, but doesn't really show absolute magnitudes well, and doesn't give a great overview across time. This really begs the question if you're making a serious tool or a educational toy.

text: The current design is that the simulator annotates values into the schematic, which are accessible to each device's template. That way each device can display its DC and AC operating point. There aren't really any embedded transient plotting facilities.

raw.full_text: The current design is that the simulator annotates values into the schematic, which are accessible to each device's template. That way each device can display its DC and AC operating point. There aren't really any embedded transient plotting facilities.

text: I think it's obvious that the template has to be replaced by some UI with sane defaults. It's less obvious to me how transient visualization should be done. falstad wires? worrydream plots? mouseover plots? Reactive notebook? Dedicated UI? This is a big one.

raw.full_text: I think it's obvious that the template has to be replaced by some UI with sane defaults. It's less obvious to me how transient visualization should be done. falstad wires? worrydream plots? mouseover plots? Reactive notebook? Dedicated UI? This is a big one.

text: NyanCAD was built on the assumption that transient simulations are big and slow. A lot of time was invested in plotting tens of thousands of datapoints efficiently. So another big question is if this is a good assumption for the target audience.

raw.full_text: NyanCAD was built on the assumption that transient simulations are big and slow. A lot of time was invested in plotting tens of thousands of datapoints efficiently. So another big question is if this is a good assumption for the target audience.

text: Really the crux of the problem is that you can't do good design by catering to everyone. A different way to put it is that NyanCAD is having an identity crisis. Do I want to target professional chip designers or hobby electronics or something else?

raw.full_text: Really the crux of the problem is that you can't do good design by catering to everyone. A different way to put it is that NyanCAD is having an identity crisis. Do I want to target professional chip designers or hobby electronics or something else?

text:
We have two more pieces to cover while we ponder the big questions: the notebook API and the simulation UI.

The idea was always to have a seamless transition between the two, where you'd do the easy stuff in the UI and then write code for advanced analysis. That never happened.

raw.full_text:
We have two more pieces to cover while we ponder the big questions: the notebook API and the simulation UI.

The idea was always to have a seamless transition between the two, where you'd do the easy stuff in the UI and then write code for advanced analysis. That never happened.

text: I think the notebook API was alright, if not a bit barren and over-engineered. There was never much in the way of analysis functions, and the simulation server idea worked but didn't add much value in practice. I might just use PySpice next time?

raw.full_text: I think the notebook API was alright, if not a bit barren and over-engineered. There was never much in the way of analysis functions, and the simulation server idea worked but didn't add much value in practice. I might just use PySpice next time?

text: The idea with the simulation server was to decouple the simulation host from the UI host, again, catering to chip designers who usually remote into a server with all the sensitive IP. But then, the notebook server already provided this decoupling.

raw.full_text: The idea with the simulation server was to decouple the simulation host from the UI host, again, catering to chip designers who usually remote into a server with all the sensitive IP. But then, the notebook server already provided this decoupling.

text: Notably, if you run the simulator in WebAssembly, you lose this decoupling. This is again fine for casual users but prohibitive for chip designers working under NDA.  Identity crisis!!!

raw.full_text: Notably, if you run the simulator in WebAssembly, you lose this decoupling. This is again fine for casual users but prohibitive for chip designers working under NDA.  Identity crisis!!!

text: The plotting UI was a pretty straightforward Panel app capable of configuring basic simulations and basic plots. It offered configuration options conspicuously absent from falstad/worrysim. Its best party trick was streaming plots of large datasets.

raw.full_text: The plotting UI was a pretty straightforward Panel app capable of configuring basic simulations and basic plots. It offered configuration options conspicuously absent from falstad/worrysim. Its best party trick was streaming plots of large datasets.

text: As mentioned this was supposed to interact with notebooks, but that never really happened. Now I wonder if it'd be better to just open the editor with a default notebook UI that runs basic simulations. https://t.co/aM0RUXbnsP

raw.full_text: As mentioned this was supposed to interact with notebooks, but that never really happened. Now I wonder if it'd be better to just open the editor with a default notebook UI that runs basic simulations. https://t.co/aM0RUXbnsP

text: Shining in absence in all of this is any sort of device library. You know what I use most for actual spice simulations? LTSpice under wine, because it has all the common parts included with many more available online. Absolutely crucial.

raw.full_text: Shining in absence in all of this is any sort of device library. You know what I use most for actual spice simulations? LTSpice under wine, because it has all the common parts included with many more available online. Absolutely crucial.

text: For chip design you just get a complete PDK with all the devices you can possibly use. For PCB level, you can use the entire Mouser catalog, devastating. I'm not sure there is an easy and/or legal way out here. Most manufacturers do publish spice models, but lots of work.

raw.full_text: For chip design you just get a complete PDK with all the devices you can possibly use. For PCB level, you can use the entire Mouser catalog, devastating. I'm not sure there is an easy and/or legal way out here. Most manufacturers do publish spice models, but lots of work.

text: I think that more or less concludes the conceptual part. TL;DR, there are lots of good parts but we're in a bit of an identity crisis. Now, onward to the technological implementation.

raw.full_text: I think that more or less concludes the conceptual part. TL;DR, there are lots of good parts but we're in a bit of an identity crisis. Now, onward to the technological implementation.

text: ClojureScript shielded me a bit from JS churn and boilerplate, but is a very stagnant community. It works well but there were also some weird things that are just not getting fixed. Async is buggy, and support for futures and JS packages is lacking, IIRC.

raw.full_text: ClojureScript shielded me a bit from JS churn and boilerplate, but is a very stagnant community. It works well but there were also some weird things that are just not getting fixed. Async is buggy, and support for futures and JS packages is lacking, IIRC.

text: CouchDB/PouchDB as a backend was a PITA deployment wise, and are also somewhat stagnant. The CouchAtom wrapper works amazing though, and solves all my storage and synchronization needs. But it's a leaky abstraction that leads to the mentioned bulk action jank.

raw.full_text: CouchDB/PouchDB as a backend was a PITA deployment wise, and are also somewhat stagnant. The CouchAtom wrapper works amazing though, and solves all my storage and synchronization needs. But it's a leaky abstraction that leads to the mentioned bulk action jank.

text: Of course there might be a performance penalty to running in webassembly, so I do think you'll want some escape hatch to run the simulator outside the browser. Or maybe the entire notebook server should be decoupled.

raw.full_text: Of course there might be a performance penalty to running in webassembly, so I do think you'll want some escape hatch to run the simulator outside the browser. Or maybe the entire notebook server should be decoupled.

text: Talking about notebook servers, JupyterLab was also kind of a pain to work with. I never really managed to properly integrate NyanCAD in a nice way, so they just kinda ran alongside each other, talking via the database.

raw.full_text: Talking about notebook servers, JupyterLab was also kind of a pain to work with. I never really managed to properly integrate NyanCAD in a nice way, so they just kinda ran alongside each other, talking via the database.

text: I have been thinking, why can't solar /wind inverters simulate inertia? Either physically by creating the electrical dual of a flywheel, or virtually by spare capacity and control systems.

raw.full_text: I have been thinking, why can't solar /wind inverters simulate inertia? Either physically by creating the electrical dual of a flywheel, or virtually by spare capacity and control systems.

text: The simplest thing to do would be to replace the inverter by a DC motor into an AC generator so you just literally have inertia but I don't think anyone wants to do that. Though if you're adding dedicated spinny bits for stability you might as well right...

raw.full_text: The simplest thing to do would be to replace the inverter by a DC motor into an AC generator so you just literally have inertia but I don't think anyone wants to do that. Though if you're adding dedicated spinny bits for stability you might as well right...

text: But you can make a bond graph of that system, take the dual, shift the domain barrier around, and Bob's your uncle. So we have gyrator, inertia, gyrator. Take the dual to get transformer, compliance, transformer. Aka mppt, capacitor bank, inverter! Just add capacitors!!

raw.full_text: But you can make a bond graph of that system, take the dual, shift the domain barrier around, and Bob's your uncle. So we have gyrator, inertia, gyrator. Take the dual to get transformer, compliance, transformer. Aka mppt, capacitor bank, inverter! Just add capacitors!!

text: I'm simplifying a bit because the inverter still needs to simulate inertia in its AC frequency but you can trivially draw that up in simulink or whatever. The energy balance remains the same. Question is how big the capacitor bank needs to be.

raw.full_text: I'm simplifying a bit because the inverter still needs to simulate inertia in its AC frequency but you can trivially draw that up in simulink or whatever. The energy balance remains the same. Question is how big the capacitor bank needs to be.

text: Maybe prohibitively large? Another option is spare capacity. You can run the mppt below peak, and catch peaks and valleys by shifting the power point around. Yes that's wasteful but not more wasteful than keeping coal plants on standby.

raw.full_text: Maybe prohibitively large? Another option is spare capacity. You can run the mppt below peak, and catch peaks and valleys by shifting the power point around. Yes that's wasteful but not more wasteful than keeping coal plants on standby.

text: I'm not sure what to make of gnucap... They seem to have much higher ambitions than ngspice but priorities that are alien to me. So I'm like... SURELY they are a better bet than using ngspice but in its current form it is just straight up useless to me.

raw.full_text: I'm not sure what to make of gnucap... They seem to have much higher ambitions than ngspice but priorities that are alien to me. So I'm like... SURELY they are a better bet than using ngspice but in its current form it is just straight up useless to me.

text: They are 100% committed to mixed signal verilog to the point they don't even support reading in spice models. Everything is a plugin in gnucap though, so I'm going to write a spice to verilog converter, I guess? That's a non-negotiable requirement to me, afterthought to them

raw.full_text: They are 100% committed to mixed signal verilog to the point they don't even support reading in spice models. Everything is a plugin in gnucap though, so I'm going to write a spice to verilog converter, I guess? That's a non-negotiable requirement to me, afterthought to them

text: We both agree ngspice sucks, but we don't agree that gnucap also sucks. They seem not that interested in learning from Xyce or CedarSim because they are not "fast spice" or mixed signal, citing some 90s paper as the state of the art.

raw.full_text: We both agree ngspice sucks, but we don't agree that gnucap also sucks. They seem not that interested in learning from Xyce or CedarSim because they are not "fast spice" or mixed signal, citing some 90s paper as the state of the art.

text: I'm in a weird position where I know a little about solvers, Xyce, and CedarSim, and almost nothing about gnucap, while they of course know everything about gnucap. So I have like a weakly held belief gnucap is missing out but not the deep knowledge to really know for sure.

raw.full_text: I'm in a weird position where I know a little about solvers, Xyce, and CedarSim, and almost nothing about gnucap, while they of course know everything about gnucap. So I have like a weakly held belief gnucap is missing out but not the deep knowledge to really know for sure.

text: I don't have any benchmarks which maybe I should, but commercial simulators are like 100x faster than ngspice, xyce 10x and gnucap 2x. CedarSim could match or exceed AFS on some circuits, but often failed entirely on bigger circuits.

raw.full_text: I don't have any benchmarks which maybe I should, but commercial simulators are like 100x faster than ngspice, xyce 10x and gnucap 2x. CedarSim could match or exceed AFS on some circuits, but often failed entirely on bigger circuits.

text: CedarSim was basically "let's apply state of the art from other physics domains to electronics" without the "here is a huge bag of tricks specific to electronics" so it was blazing fast if it worked at all.

raw.full_text: CedarSim was basically "let's apply state of the art from other physics domains to electronics" without the "here is a huge bag of tricks specific to electronics" so it was blazing fast if it worked at all.

text: So I'm kinda like... hey we used this solver that was really good, maybe you should consider using it. But also, maybe that solver wasn't super robust for circuits or maybe that was only a minor aspect of our performance compared to structural analysis of the circuit.

raw.full_text: So I'm kinda like... hey we used this solver that was really good, maybe you should consider using it. But also, maybe that solver wasn't super robust for circuits or maybe that was only a minor aspect of our performance compared to structural analysis of the circuit.

text: So idk, part of me wants to try and make gnucap 10x faster, part of me wants to just let them cook and take the improvement over ngspice, part of me has the ick and wants nothing to do with it

raw.full_text: So idk, part of me wants to try and make gnucap 10x faster, part of me wants to just let them cook and take the improvement over ngspice, part of me has the ick and wants nothing to do with it

text: @ollyrobot @djmicrobeads @__justplaying Why are you being obtuse. Of course I have a rough idea of the value, as well as several people bidding but nobody actually pulling through. I want a good price but if nobody pulls through I'll have to lower it and if nobody would buy it at all yes I'd give it away or trash it.

text: @ollyrobot @djmicrobeads @__justplaying I'm basically doing demand based pricing. Try asking an airline what their minimum price is. I'm sure it technically exists at any given time based on fuel costs bla bla but it's not a number you or your sales rep have access to.

text: @ollyrobot @djmicrobeads @__justplaying The social contract on this marketplace is that you bid a bit under the list price and go back and forth until both are happy. text:
Me: I want to run LLMs on my GPU raspi
Ollama: we don't care about Vulkan
AMD: we don't care about ARM
Home Assistant: we don't care about llama.cpp
I made it work, but it would be a lot easier if any of the above parties did care.

raw.full_text:
Me: I want to run LLMs on my GPU raspi
Ollama: we don't care about Vulkan
AMD: we don't care about ARM
Home Assistant: we don't care about llama.cpp
I made it work, but it would be a lot easier if any of the above parties did care.

text: Home Assistant supports Ollama and OpenAI but closes any PRs for local OpenAI-compatible software like vLLM or llama.cpp. Ollama only supports CUDA and ROCm and have been ignoring and rejecting other backends. AMD simply doesn't support ROCm on ARM. Okay now what?

raw.full_text: Home Assistant supports Ollama and OpenAI but closes any PRs for local OpenAI-compatible software like vLLM or llama.cpp. Ollama only supports CUDA and ROCm and have been ignoring and rejecting other backends. AMD simply doesn't support ROCm on ARM. Okay now what?

text: llama.cpp supports Vulkan which does support ARM, and the third party Advanced OpenAI Conversations does support changing the base URL, so that combination works well enough, although it is lagging behind HA changes a bit.

raw.full_text: llama.cpp supports Vulkan which does support ARM, and the third party Advanced OpenAI Conversations does support changing the base URL, so that combination works well enough, although it is lagging behind HA changes a bit.

text: I've heard rumors that Debian is patching ROCm for ARM but it doesn't appear to be in their repos. If that worked I could just run Ollama. Or if Ollama supported Vulkan. Or if Home Assistant allowed changing the OpenAI base URL. sigh

raw.full_text: I've heard rumors that Debian is patching ROCm for ARM but it doesn't appear to be in their repos. If that worked I could just run Ollama. Or if Ollama supported Vulkan. Or if Home Assistant allowed changing the OpenAI base URL. sigh

text: I'm curious, if you have a robot vacuum, how often do you run it and how much effort do you put in clearing the floor? We used to do weekly and remove all the chairs, but with the new self-emptying one, low effort daily seems attractive.

raw.full_text: I'm curious, if you have a robot vacuum, how often do you run it and how much effort do you put in clearing the floor? We used to do weekly and remove all the chairs, but with the new self-emptying one, low effort daily seems attractive.

text: I guess my thought is if you need to babysit it anyway to empty the bin and remove cables you might as well clear the floor completely but doing that daily is a chore. But if it doesn't choke on cables and empties itself there is little downside to running it daily.

raw.full_text: I guess my thought is if you need to babysit it anyway to empty the bin and remove cables you might as well clear the floor completely but doing that daily is a chore. But if it doesn't choke on cables and empties itself there is little downside to running it daily.

text: And if you run it daily, well, it's not so bad if it misses a few spots. As long as it's not the same spot every day. Everything that regularly changes location should be fine eventually, but you might still want to clear the floor occasionally for semi-permanent items.

text: Manic Miners is a fan remake of Lego Rock Raiders, a unique '90s Lego game about the Lego theme with the same name. It's basically an RTS where you build a base and units but it's mostly about economy and puzzles, with the occasional rock monster or slimy slug attacking you.

raw.full_text: Manic Miners is a fan remake of Lego Rock Raiders, a unique '90s Lego game about the Lego theme with the same name. It's basically an RTS where you build a base and units but it's mostly about economy and puzzles, with the occasional rock monster or slimy slug attacking you.

text: It's a very unique game that we played a lot. We also have almost all the Lego sets. But as it tends to go with old games they sometimes age poorly. But then some madlad recreated the whole game in Unreal Engine and then went on to work for Lego. What a legend.

raw.full_text: It's a very unique game that we played a lot. We also have almost all the Lego sets. But as it tends to go with old games they sometimes age poorly. But then some madlad recreated the whole game in Unreal Engine and then went on to work for Lego. What a legend.

text: And it has a level editor and active discord community. As it tends to go with my gaming, I quickly shift into building for the game more than playing it. They have a scripting language!!

raw.full_text: And it has a level editor and active discord community. As it tends to go with my gaming, I quickly shift into building for the game more than playing it. They have a scripting language!!

text: The scripting language is more like assembly than Lua, just my perverse kind of fun. I've implemented lights out and labyrinth in it and am currently working on a 4D puzzle level that rewrites the map as you play.

text: Arguably ngspice is a better spice parser but you can't really use it as a library to analyze and manipulate spice code. Other libraries like PySpice are toys in comparison and even other simulators like GnuCap and Xyce don't try very hard to be compatible with various spices

text: I could just try to replicate all the transformations that ngspice and xyce support to have it all under one roof. Maybe there is some use for that but it feels a bit NIH.

raw.full_text: I could just try to replicate all the transformations that ngspice and xyce support to have it all under one roof. Maybe there is some use for that but it feels a bit NIH.

text: I could try to focus on transformations ngspice DOESN'T support, but what are they, and who cares about them? If I had a specific PDK or model that I wanted to run I could just have that as a goal but I don't. Can I find someone with a problem to solve?

raw.full_text: I could try to focus on transformations ngspice DOESN'T support, but what are they, and who cares about them? If I had a specific PDK or model that I wanted to run I could just have that as a goal but I don't. Can I find someone with a problem to solve?

text: The original plan was actually to convert spice to Verilog-A. That would be really cool if you had a really good Verilog-A simulator, right?! Nngspice and VACASK both use OpenVAF which seems on maintenance mode. Gnucap is doing their own weird thing.

raw.full_text: The original plan was actually to convert spice to Verilog-A. That would be really cool if you had a really good Verilog-A simulator, right?! Nngspice and VACASK both use OpenVAF which seems on maintenance mode. Gnucap is doing their own weird thing.

text: So I went from wanting to pave the way for the glorious future of Verilog-A, realizing how far away that future is, pivoting to converting spice dialects, realizing ngspice/xyce are already doing a lot of that, to not really having a goal with my parser adventures.

text: A thing llms are much worse at than I expect is very mechanical very large tasks like "rewrite this python in C++" or "port this build system from automake to cmake" and I don't really understand why. It's a fuzzy 1:1 translation but they just give up and half ass it.

raw.full_text: A thing llms are much worse at than I expect is very mechanical very large tasks like "rewrite this python in C++" or "port this build system from automake to cmake" and I don't really understand why. It's a fuzzy 1:1 translation but they just give up and half ass it.

text: Everyone is saying "use the right tool for the job" but I feel programming languages are converging. I would describe it as gradually typed soft functional C with interfaces, of which notable forerunners are Julia, Rust, and Typescript.

raw.full_text: Everyone is saying "use the right tool for the job" but I feel programming languages are converging. I would describe it as gradually typed soft functional C with interfaces, of which notable forerunners are Julia, Rust, and Typescript.

text: It turns out static types are a good idea but nobody wants to write elaborate type definitions. Type inference let's you have your cake and  eat it too. Seems like no brainer.

raw.full_text: It turns out static types are a good idea but nobody wants to write elaborate type definitions. Type inference let's you have your cake and  eat it too. Seems like no brainer.

text: Similarly, functional programming is a good idea but nobody wants to write elaborate map reduce algorithms. Optional mutability and strong types get you most of the benefits on top of easy IO and loops.

raw.full_text: Similarly, functional programming is a good idea but nobody wants to write elaborate map reduce algorithms. Optional mutability and strong types get you most of the benefits on top of easy IO and loops.

text: Polymorphism is a good idea but nobody wants to deal with deep inheritance chains. Type dispatch on structs with interfaces gets you most of the OOP benefits without the "design patterns"

raw.full_text: Polymorphism is a good idea but nobody wants to deal with deep inheritance chains. Type dispatch on structs with interfaces gets you most of the OOP benefits without the "design patterns"

text: Memory management is still divided. Will we ever see gradual borrow checking? Borrow checking is great but nobody wants to annotate the type of reference they hold. Gradual borrow checking lets you infer clear access patterns and GC shared state.

raw.full_text: Memory management is still divided. Will we ever see gradual borrow checking? Borrow checking is great but nobody wants to annotate the type of reference they hold. Gradual borrow checking lets you infer clear access patterns and GC shared state.

text: With JIT, interpreted vs compiled is a fading distinction but we haven't quite closed the gap yet. Julia tried but came up short, the aot ergonomics aren't there yet and they made choices that make it hard.

raw.full_text: With JIT, interpreted vs compiled is a fading distinction but we haven't quite closed the gap yet. Julia tried but came up short, the aot ergonomics aren't there yet and they made choices that make it hard.

text:
@QuentinTheDot An FPGA is a piece of reconfigurable hardware
Gowin is a brand of FPGAs 
Apicula is an open source toolchain for programming Gowin FPGAs that I wrote
Attosoc configures the FPGA as a RISC-V CPU computing primes
The packer is the last part of the toolchain
It's faster now

raw.full_text:
@QuentinTheDot An FPGA is a piece of reconfigurable hardware
Gowin is a brand of FPGAs 
Apicula is an open source toolchain for programming Gowin FPGAs that I wrote
Attosoc configures the FPGA as a RISC-V CPU computing primes
The packer is the last part of the toolchain
It's faster now

text:
@QuentinTheDot Nextpnr is a place and route tool, it takes a list of FPGA primitives and their connections, and then maps them to concrete resources inside the FPGA.
Inside an FPGA is a grid of tiles, each tile has lookup tables, flip flops, and a lot of multiplexers connecting to neighbours.

raw.full_text:
@QuentinTheDot Nextpnr is a place and route tool, it takes a list of FPGA primitives and their connections, and then maps them to concrete resources inside the FPGA.
Inside an FPGA is a grid of tiles, each tile has lookup tables, flip flops, and a lot of multiplexers connecting to neighbours.

text: @QuentinTheDot And finally, there is the packer, which takes the result of nextpnr (primitives and their location) and generates a bitstream, which is the actual file you upload to the FPGA that configures each primitive.

raw.full_text: @QuentinTheDot And finally, there is the packer, which takes the result of nextpnr (primitives and their location) and generates a bitstream, which is the actual file you upload to the FPGA that configures each primitive.

text:
my new favorite Dutch language feature: where English slaps "-er" on a word (joker), Dutch reaches for a random object:
grapjas (joke jacket)
brompot (grumble pot)
kletskous (chat stocking)
angsthaas (fear hare)
bofkont (luck butt)
zuurpruim (sour plum)
pechvogel (bad luck bird)

my new favorite Dutch language feature: where English slaps "-er" on a word (joker), Dutch reaches for a random object:
grapjas (joke jacket)
brompot (grumble pot)
kletskous (chat stocking)
angsthaas (fear hare)
bofkont (luck butt)
zuurpruim (sour plum)
pechvogel (bad luck bird)

text:
German does this too but not always with the same objects
grapjas → Spaßvogel (fun bird)
bofkont → Glückspilz (luck mushroom)
brompot → Brummbär (grumble bear)
angsthaas → Angsthase (same!)
pechvogel → Pechvogel (same!)

text: A differential algebraic equation is the type of system that electrical circuits produces, with capacitors and indicators for the differential part and resistors and sources for the algebraic part. It's what Cadnip simulates.

raw.full_text: A differential algebraic equation is the type of system that electrical circuits produces, with capacitors and indicators for the differential part and resistors and sources for the algebraic part. It's what Cadnip simulates.

text: ESDIRK are a type of implicit Runge kutta method which means they use a nonlinear solve, which is good for circuits. But the E means the first part of it is explicit and that makes them unsuitable for DAEs.

raw.full_text: ESDIRK are a type of implicit Runge kutta method which means they use a nonlinear solve, which is good for circuits. But the E means the first part of it is explicit and that makes them unsuitable for DAEs.

text: First same as last is a subtype of those where the first bit is the same as the last which means you can reuse the last bit as the first bit for the next step. That unlocks solving DAEs with them, which the library Cadnip uses now supports.

raw.full_text: First same as last is a subtype of those where the first bit is the same as the last which means you can reuse the last bit as the first bit for the next step. That unlocks solving DAEs with them, which the library Cadnip uses now supports.

text: So each tile will be laser printed and fitted with precision metal dowels, but rather than screwing down the rails with some alignment jig I'm thinking of just adding tabs to the module and having _loose_ ends with normal rail joiners for the last microns of flex.

raw.full_text: So each tile will be laser printed and fitted with precision metal dowels, but rather than screwing down the rails with some alignment jig I'm thinking of just adding tabs to the module and having _loose_ ends with normal rail joiners for the last microns of flex.
